coredata.dc:title: On the mechanism of dendritic fragmentation by ultrasound induced cavitation
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, S.
Guo, Z.P.
Zhang, X.P.
Zhang, A.
Kang, J.W.
coredata.dc:description: A dedicated solidification device and high speed camera were used to capture dendritic fragmentation of pure succinonitrile (SCN) induced by oscillating ultrasonic bubbles. Theoretical analysis of the melting behavior of the dendrite was performed based on local solidification thermodynamics. The dendritic growth or the evolution of the solid-liquid interface is closely related to both thermodynamics of the cavitation bubble and the local geometry of the dendrite. Accordingly, for the first time, a dimensionless scaling formulation was developed by fitting both theoretical and experimental data to determine the variational pressure exerted by the cavitation bubble.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dendritic fragmentation
Ultrasound
Cavitation bubble
Acoustic pressure

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The oscillation of cavitation bubble near solid boundary
The pressure exerted by the cavitation bubble

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Configuration of the experimental setup. (a) Shows a schematic illustration of the setup viewed from top whereas (b) shows the cavitation bubbles generated by ultrasound probe near the growing dendrites.

(a) and (b) show the oscillation of a single cavitation bubble captured at 80,000 fps near a concave spherical dendrite boundary (P and S in a1 are used to designate two regions of interest on the dendrite). (c) Shows high-speed micro-jets at the bubble surface developed during the oscillation of cavitation bubble in experiment. (c1–c3) were three continuous images showing the jet evolution and (c4–c8) were some individual images of jet for cavitation bubble.

(a) Melting of a SCN dendrite in contact with a cavitation bubble. The melting process can be categorized into two types, as characterized by “L1” and “L2”, which are further characterized by R1 and R2 respectively. (b) and (c) show the evolution of L1 and L2. (d) and (e) show a schematic illustration of the melting process for L1 and L2.

Melting behavior based on the solutions of Eq. (12) with various

.

1

according to model I (see Fig. 4b and d). The black solid squares represent the experiment results whereas the blue line and the red line demonstrate the cases without and with the influence of the cavitation pressure. (b) Theoretical prediction of
R ¯
1
versus time under different cavitation pressure. The dots (with the same color) show the corresponding melting rate. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The evolution of

according to model II (see Fig. 4c and e). The blue line demonstrates the theoretical prediction of the

in experiment. The red line demonstrated the modified theoretical prediction by accounting for the newly estimated cavitation pressure based on Eq. (10). (b) Shows the evolution of the diameter in both experiment and theoretical prediction versus

. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Geometry parameters for models I and II without the influence of cavitation.

head.ce:title: On the mechanism of dendritic fragmentation by ultrasound induced cavitation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials Processing Technology, Ministry of Education, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials Processing Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence authors at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing, China (Z.P. Guo, J.W. Kang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dedicated solidification device and high speed camera were used to capture dendritic fragmentation of pure succinonitrile (SCN) induced by oscillating ultrasonic bubbles. Theoretical analysis of the melting behavior of the dendrite was performed based on local solidification thermodynamics. The dendritic growth or the evolution of the solid-liquid interface is closely related to both thermodynamics of the cavitation bubble and the local geometry of the dendrite. Accordingly, for the first time, a dimensionless scaling formulation was developed by fitting both theoretical and experimental data to determine the variational pressure exerted by the cavitation bubble.

ce:section.ce:para: Power ultrasound is an effective way to induce microstructure refinement during solidification by fragmenting the primary phase, i.e., dendrites [1–5]. According to Abramov et al. [6] and Eskin [7], the oscillation of cavitation bubble was the major mechanism for grain refinement. The cavitation bubbles oscillated under the continuous ultrasonic waves, and generated a pressure gradient between the far-field fluid and the bubble surface [8]. The high pressure produced by the intense oscillation or collapse of cavitation was the reason for grain refinement of crystals.

In-situ experiment was performed to observe dendrite fragmentation induced by ultrasound for both analogue [9–12] and metallic alloys [13–16]. Chow et al. [11,12] observed the fragmentation of ice dendrites induced by cavitation. Shu et al. and Wang et al. observed detailed interaction between bubble and SCN dendrite with higher imaging speed [9,10]. The dendritic fragmentation was attributed to the forces or pressure generated by cavitation bubble.

Tae-Hong et al. [17] captured the dynamic behavior of individual ultrasonic bubble and analyzed the interface motion and interaction with solid structures. The pressure of the cavitation bubble with different oscillation modes were detected by microcantilevers. Ananiev et al. [18] developed a model to demonstrate the catastrophic elastic remelting of dendrite under applied forces. Accordingly, the pressure induced by cavitation bubble can be regarded as an external force which subsequently led to the remelting of dendrites.

In this paper, the fragmentation behavior of a SCN dendrite induced by cavitation bubbles was captured and studied. The experiment was performed using an ultrasound with a power of 30 W and an image capturing rate of 80,000 fps. The energy (pressure) exerted by oscillating bubbles was estimated by investigating the melting behavior of the SCN dendrite. A theoretical model was developed to reveal the underlying fragmentation mechanism based on interaction between the cavitation bubble and the microstructure.

Fig. 1 shows a schematical illustration of the experimental setup. Two connected glass chambers were designed to investigate the cavitation effect [9,10]. The left chamber (15 mm × 20 mm × 0.3 mm) was thinner and used for accommodating the dendrite, whereas the right one (15 mm × 20 mm × 3 mm) was used for locating the ultrasound probe (2 mm in diameter). The succinonitrile (SCN) was used as the solidification material whose melting point is 331 K and other thermodynamical properties are listed in Table 1
[19–22]. During experiment, the temperature gradient was set to be 1.666 K/mm, which was equivalent to the case with a temperature of TH = 65 °C at the hotter side and TL = 40 °C at the cooler side. The solidification microstructure grew from left side to right of the domain and the dendritic front was controlled to be at the center of the left domain for observation [10]. An ultrasound device namely SONICS VCX 150 was employed with a power of 30 W and frequency of 20 kHz. A high-speed digital video camera (Nac HX-6) with an optical microscope and ∼800 times magnification was used for image recording. Each image had 128 × 128 pixels and the acquisition rate was 80,000 fps, which were four times faster than that of the ultrasound frequency, i.e., 20 kHz. During experiment, the ultrasound power was turned on for only one second to generate cavitation bubbles near the dendrites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The oscillation of cavitation bubble near solid boundary
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the oscillation of a single cavitation bubble near a curved dendrite boundary. In Fig. 2a1, a cavitation bubble of ∼150 μm exhibited near a partially melted dendrite with a wall thickness of ∼200 μm. Two parts of the dendrites were designated by P and S respectively. Fig. 2a–c show typical oscillation periods of cavitation bubble at different stages. The oscillation was mainly a repetition of expansion and contraction of the volume. In principle, the oscillation period of the cavitation bubble lasted for about four image frames, i.e., 50 microseconds. However, in Fig. 2a and b, the periodic oscillation comprised two different modes (see Fig. 2a1–a8 and b1–b8).

For the first four frames, the area “A” against the dendrite boundary (see Fig. 2a1) expanded while the area “B” contracted. However, during the subsequent four frames of evolution, the oscillation mode reversed, i.e., “A” contracted while “B” expanded. Similar oscillation modes can be seen in [23]. It was believed that such behavior of the bubble was caused by the periodic positive and negative pressure induced by ultrasound [24]. The cavitation bubble oscillated in a rather stable mode, and did not collapse during all observed periods. The bubble exhibited a non-spherical shape due to its interaction with a nearby solid boundary, i.e., the dendrite. The melting of primary arm can be observed by comparing Fig. 2a and b. As designated by the arrow in Fig. 2b1, the secondary arm fragmented as the bubble oscillated. The re-entrant jet of the bubble wall was also observed in Fig. 2a3–a4, and b3–b4, which was similar as the initial stage of the collapsing bubble near boundary [17,25–28].

Fig. 2c shows some particular morphological evolution at the bubble surface during the oscillation of a cavitation bubble. Fig. 2c1–c3 show the continuous evolution of a jet flow at the bubble surface against the crystal boundary. Similar jet flows can also be found in [29–31]. Fig. 2c4 shows a broken jet [31] after the jet moving backwards from the bubble surface. A globular bubble can be clearly seen inside the cavitation bubble (see Fig. 2c4). Fig. 2c5 shows a butterfly-shape cavitation bubble as a re-entrant was generated and moved inside the bubble center. A jet flow formed near the bubble surface, which consequently generated an extra pressure on the solidifying crystal. Fig. 2c6 shows two symmetric jets as designated by arrows. Besides, inverse jets pointing to the free liquid could also be observed (see in Fig. 2c7–c8), which was similar to these found in [17].

To quantify the melting behavior, we located and chose one interacting scenario in which both oscillation bubble and dendrite were present (see Fig. 3 a). The oscillation of the bubble led to the melting of a neighboring dendrite. The melting or fragmentation could be further categorized into two types. The first was the direct contact between the dendrite and the cavitation bubble, as designated by “L1”, while the second was caused by an indirect contact between the two, as designated by “L2”. The curve “L1” moved leftwards as the cavitation bubble oscillated, indicating a local melting (see Fig. 3a). The part designated by “L2” became fragmented at ∼1.145 s. The evolution of “L1” and “L2” was characterized by the distance R1 and R2 (see Fig. 3a-0.5 s). The evolution of both curves, i.e., “L1” and “L2” was extracted using a software namely DataDig5, and shown by dots in Fig. 3b and c. These data points were further fitted using a regression method, and results showed that the fitted curves were parabolic for both cases. Accordingly, two separate models were developed to characterize the evolution, detail of which was shown in Fig. 3d and e.

For both models, the evolution of the solid surface could be characterized by a parabola:

If
1 - f / 2 < 0
, Eq. (12) does not have a real solution. Fig. 4 a shows one of the examples according to this case, i.e., f = 4, in which the dendrite shrinks until fragmentation occurs. Besides, variation of the initial
R ¯
, i.e.,
R ¯
0
only affects the melting rate. If f = 2 or
1 - f / 2 = 0
, Eq. (12) only has one real solution, i.e.
R ¯
= 1
. Fig. 4b shows that in this case,
R ¯

R ¯

. Fig. 4c and d show a typical situation for this case where f = 0.72. Accordingly,
R ¯

and

R ¯

(see Fig. 4d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pressure exerted by the cavitation bubble
ce:section.ce:para:
Table 2 lists the parameters for models I and II (see Fig. 3d and e) in experiment assuming that no external forces existed. According to the geometry of model I (see Fig. 3d),
1 - f / 2 > 0
, and
R ¯
min
<
R ¯
0
<
R ¯
max
, so the grain boundary would grow to
R ¯
max
. For model II (see Fig. 3e), f = 4.54, and
1 - f / 2 < 0
, so the solid surface would shrink until fragmentation occurred. We solved Eq. (11) numerically using the initial parameters listed in Table 2 for models I and II.

Fig. 5 a shows the melting/growth behavior of the dendrite wall with and without the influence of cavitation pressure for both experiment and theoretical predictions. As shown by the blue line, without the influence of cavitation pressure dendrite tended to grow exponentially as time instead of shrinking. The maximum radius of the dendrite tended to approach
R ¯
max
= 1.79
as predicted in Table 2. However, in experiment (see the black solid square), the dendrite wall shrank linearly versus
t ¯
until
R ¯
approached 0.55, indicating that the cavitation pressure significantly influenced the melting/growth behavior of the dendrite. According to Eq. (12), the net effect of the cavitation pressure, i.e.,
Δ
P ¯
could be estimated based on the slope difference of the two datasets, i.e., from experiment and theoretical prediction. Accordingly,
Δ
P ¯
= − 495, which was about − 33.5 kPa based on the current material and experiment setup. The estimated pressure was on the same magnitude of that in [17] for cavitation bubble. Adding the estimated
Δ
P ¯
in Eq. (10), the evolution of
R ¯
1
versus
t ¯
was shown as the red line in Fig. 5a. This evolution was in perfect match with that of the experiment.

Fig. 5b showed the theoretical prediction of
R ¯
1
versus time and the melting rate under different cavitation pressure. It is clear that the melting rate increased as
Δ
P
¯
increased. When
Δ
P ¯
> - 100
, the melting rate was relatively low whereas when
Δ
P ¯
< - 100
, the melting rate increased abruptly. The different changing behavior of
R ¯
1
before and after
Δ
P ¯
= - 100
demonstrated the significance of the curvature effect.

Fig. 6 a shows the evolution of the neck radius, i.e.,
R ¯
2
for the second case, i.e. model II (see Fig. 4c and e). For the whole duration of the experiment,
R ¯
2
decreased until fragmentation occurred at
t ¯
= 0.02
. However, theoretical prediction according to Eq. (11) showed that the fragmentation occurred at
t ¯
= 0.3
(see the blue line in Fig. 6a), which was much longer than that in experiment. On the other hand, for both experiment and theoretical prediction,
R ¯
2
decreased nonlinearly versus time, i.e., slowly at first but abruptly afterwards. Such nonlinearity was attributed to the curvature effect when
R ¯
2
< 0.3
. For the initial stage of the data,
R ¯
2
changed in a rather linear manner and through linear regression the net effect of the external force, i.e.,
Δ
P ¯
could be estimated based on the slope difference between the two datasets according to Eq. (12). In this respect,
Δ
P ¯
= - 35
or −10.2 kPa. It is noted for this case, the melting location was 100 μm away from bubble center, the pressure amplitude was lower than that for model I. Taking the pressure influence into account, a modified theoretical prediction was shown by the red line, which agreed quite well with that in experiment.

It is clear that the presence of the cavitation pressure altered the melting rate of the dendrite arm. Fig. 6b shows the evolution of the dimensionless diameter of the arm neck versus
t ¯
-
t s
¯
1 / 3
. The fitting goodness of the data clearly demonstrated that the diameter of the arm neck changed in a self-similar manner, i.e.,
r ∼
α L
d 0
t
¯
-
t
¯
s
1 / 3
which was similar as that found in [37]. In this equation, d 0 is the capillary length and
t s
¯
is the nondimensional time when the necking effect started to occur. In addition, by accounting for the pressure effect, i.e.,
Δ
P ¯
= - 35
, the experimental data could be perfectly described using Eq. (10).

In this paper, dendritic fragmentation or dendritic melting induced by ultrasonic cavitation was captured using a high speed camera. Particular attention was focused on the interaction thermodynamics between a cavitation bubble and a solid dendrite wall. For the first time a dimensionless scaling formulation, i.e.,
d
R ¯
d
t ¯
= -
κ ¯
1
R ¯
-
κ ¯
- Δ
P ¯
was developed to determine the variational pressure exerted by the cavitation bubble. Based on the theoretical and experiment results, a typical bubble with a size of 150 μm could generate a pressure of about −30 kPa at bubble wall. The pressure decreased to about −10 kPa at a location about 100 μm away from the bubble center. Geometry parameters of the dendrite before contacting the bubble, including the shape factor and the initial radius, could significantly influence the melting or growth behavior of the dendrite. The oscillation of the cavitation bubble, on the other hand, exerted an additional pressure effect, which could in turn accelerate the melting of the dendrite, leading to dendritic fragmentation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51075299). Dr. Guo would like to thank the UK Royal Society for financial support via the Newton International Fellowship Scheme.
